raw_response:
## CC3.1 Readiness Assessment Findings — Q4 2025

| # | Finding Title | Related Document / Section | Why It’s a Gap | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objectives are not clearly mapped to identified risks | Risk Management Policy §5 Risk Appetite; Annual Risk Assessment Report §1–§4; Risk Register | CC3.1 requires the entity to specify objectives with sufficient clarity to identify risks to achieving those objectives. The policy defines general risk appetite statements, such as low appetite for information security, zero tolerance for known PHI exposure, and 99.95% availability, but the annual assessment and risk register do not explicitly map risks to business, security, availability, privacy, compliance, or clinical data integrity objectives. Without this linkage, it is harder to demonstrate that risk identification is driven by clearly defined objectives rather than by a general threat list. | Medium |
| 2 | Annual risk assessment scope does not fully align to policy-defined domains | Risk Management Policy §7 Annual Risk Assessment; Annual Risk Assessment Report §3 Risk Categories Assessed | The policy requires the annual risk assessment to cover all risk domains, including information security, HIPAA/regulatory compliance, technology and infrastructure, third-party/vendor risk, clinical data integrity, and operational resilience. The 2025 assessment lists only five categories and does not include “clinical data integrity” as a standalone assessed domain, although one related risk is discussed under Technology. This creates a scope alignment issue between policy requirements and the documented annual assessment. | Low |
| 3 | Enterprise-wide risk coverage is not fully evidenced | Annual Risk Assessment Report §5 Risks NOT Assessed; HIPAA Security Rule Risk Analysis Summary — ePHI Inventory | The assessment excludes financial risk because it is covered by a separate CFO program, but no evidence was provided showing integration or reporting of that program into the enterprise risk management process. Additionally, the HIPAA risk analysis identifies several systems containing ePHI — including Claims Processing, Patient Portal, Analytics Pipeline, and FHIR API — but the enterprise risk register does not clearly map risks to each system or demonstrate that system-specific risks were analyzed. This limits evidence that risks are identified “across the entity.” | Medium |
| 4 | Third-party risk assessment coverage remains incomplete | Annual Risk Assessment Report §4.4 and §6 Recommendation 3; Risk Register SH-RISK-006; HIPAA Security Rule Risk Analysis Summary — Key Controls | The annual assessment identified that only 80% of business associate assessments were completed, and the Q4 risk register still states “8 of 10 BA assessments completed for 2025.” For a healthcare EHR platform processing PHI, incomplete assessment of critical business associates limits Skyline’s ability to fully identify and analyze third-party risks to PHI confidentiality, availability, and compliance obligations. | Medium |
| 5 | Risk governance activity during Q4 is not evidenced | Risk Management Policy §2.1 Risk Committee; Annual Risk Assessment Report Approval | The policy requires the Risk Committee to meet quarterly. The provided evidence shows approval of the annual risk assessment in March 2025, but no Q4 2025 Risk Committee meeting minutes, agenda, attendance record, risk review package, or action-item tracking were provided. As a result, operating effectiveness of quarterly governance and oversight during the Q4 observation period is not fully supported. | Medium |
| 6 | Risk register lacks documented change history and rationale for ratings | Risk Management Policy §3.1 Required Fields; Risk Register | The policy requires the risk register to include change history. The provided register includes notes and review dates, but it does not include a clear change history showing when ratings, owners, treatment plans, or statuses changed. It also does not document rationale for likelihood, impact, or risk rating determinations. This weakens evidence that risks are consistently analyzed and updated over time. | Low |
| 7 | HIPAA risk analysis findings are not clearly integrated into the enterprise risk process | HIPAA Security Rule Risk Analysis Summary — Findings; Annual Risk Assessment Report §6 Recommendations; Risk Register | The HIPAA risk analysis identified two recommendations: updating workforce training for social engineering and implementing automated ePHI discovery scanning for shadow IT detection. These items do not appear to be tracked in the annual risk assessment recommendations or the active risk register. While the HIPAA analysis is not by itself sufficient evidence for CC3.1, its findings should be traceable into the broader enterprise risk management process where relevant. | Low |
